environmental_storytelling.example: Apple (1687 Cambridge) placed in Bowser's Museum signals history theft; world-state corruption made visible through displaced object
Distinct visual styles for each time period (80 B.C., 776 B.C., 1520, 1602, 1687, 1879, 1903, 1905, 1947, 1989) communicate temporal context without dialogue
Cleopatra, Samurai, Albert Einstein sprites signal their historical era and relevance to artifacts
Bowser's castle interior housing historical artifacts visualizes antagonist's crime and the game's central conflict
Static text blocks scattered in time periods provide hints and lore without interrupting exploration flow
Artifacts visible/invisible depending on whether they've been returned; tracks player progress through world-state changes

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Side-scrolling platforming movement; can jump, move left/right; conversing with NPCs
Surfs through ocean minigame; collects mushrooms to power Timulator transition
Sent back to 1993 hub after 2 wrong answers; artifact mission resets
Cannot take damage or lose lives; cannot reach game over state; focuses on puzzle-solving over survival
Steals artifacts and Timulator; escapes with machine at game's end
Engaged in boss fight after player answers 3 historical questions correctly
Timulator overloads; Bowser trapped in Jurassic era; world-state restored
Provide dialogue with missing-term documents; offer hints about artifact origins
Held captive; freed upon game completion

notes: Mario's Time Machine uses artifact displacement as primary world-state signal: objects out of historical context communicate world corruption visually without explicit exposition. The museum hub serves as narrative anchor—a safe space where theft is made visible. Period-specific tile sets and NPC costumes create temporal identity without dialogue. The NES version emphasizes platforming and exploration over point-and-click puzzle-solving, making behavioral tells more action-oriented. Invulnerability mechanic signals educational game design (focus on learning, not survival). Ending outcome variation (Paradise escape vs. Jurassic trap) provides meaningful narrative consequences for player performance. Time travel minigame (surfing for mushrooms) serves as transition ritual, marking state change between eras.
